reasoning:
The question requires fatalities in South Africa, as recorded by ACLED, to exceed 10 times the average 30-day fatality count observed over the 360 days preceding March 16, 2025. With a recent baseline value of around 20 (meaning ~19 fatalities per 30 days on average), achieving a 10x threshold demands roughly >201 ACLED-recorded fatalities in a single 30-day period.

ACLED only records political violence, riots, protests, and organized armed group violence. It excludes the general criminal homicides that cause South Africa's high national murder rate. Reaching >201 political violence fatalities in 30 days is exceptionally rare and has historically only occurred during massive, unprecedented black swan events, most notably the July 2021 unrest following Jacob Zuma's imprisonment (which resulted in over 350 deaths).

Currently, there are no structural triggers of this magnitude expected in the forecast window; national elections happened in May 2024 without catastrophic violence, and the next local elections are in 2026. While service delivery protests, taxi violence, and localized xenophobic clashes are steady components of South Africa’s conflict landscape, they virtually never reach the ~200 deaths/month threshold. Given the complete absence of massive fatal riots in late February and early March 2025, the near-term probabilities are pinned to the lowest allowable boundary of 0.05. Probabilities steadily increase with extending time horizons to reflect the growing uncertainty of unforeseeable crises over the next year.
